critic_raw:
Round 1 Critique:

1. (global_auc_not_decision_metric) ROC-AUC aggregates performance over all thresholds. The retention team operates at a fixed 2% capacity. AUC gains outside the top 2% are operationally meaningless. The metric does not address the deployment question.

2. (no_capacity_constrained_evaluation) No precision@2%, recall@2%, or lift@2% is reported. These are computable from the test set and directly answer whether the model improvement matters at the actual operating point.

3. (prediction_metric_not_intervention_metric) No incremental retention rate is reported for contacted users. Ranking improvement is a proxy; retention is the outcome.
defender_raw:
Round 1 Defense:

1. AUC is a principled summary of discrimination ability. A 7-point improvement is substantial evidence of overall quality improvement, which reliably translates to top-k gains in practice.

2. Top-k metrics are computable from existing data. The omission is a gap in the write-up, not in the model. Computing precision@2% from the test set is trivial and the paper can be supplemented.

3. Incremental retention requires experimental design (holdout group not called). The test month data may not support causal estimation of retention lift without such design. The model evaluation is scoped to ranking quality, not full causal impact.
adjudication_raw:
Resolution after Round 1:

Point 1 (global_auc_not_decision_metric): OPEN — defender concedes top-k is more directly relevant but argues AUC improvement usually implies top-k improvement. This is an empirical claim, not a logical guarantee. Needs empirical resolution.

Point 2 (no_capacity_constrained_evaluation): PARTIALLY RESOLVED — defender acknowledges the gap and notes it is easily filled. The point is acknowledged; whether the gap matters depends on Point 1 resolution. Mark as empirical_test_required (compute the metrics).

Point 3 (prediction_metric_not_intervention_metric): OPEN — defender raises a valid methodological point: causal retention lift requires experimental design (holdout group). The paper cannot necessarily provide this from test-month data alone. However, it could be estimated from partial data if any cohort was withheld from calling.

Round 2:

Critic Mode 2 on Point 1: The 'AUC usually implies top-k' argument is based on general experience, not specific evidence for this model or this business context. Churn models are known cases where this relationship can fail because churners are concentrated in specific behavioral clusters that may improve or worsen at the top slice independently of global AUC.

Defender Mode 2 on Point 1: The specific mention of churn clustering as a failure mode is not supported by the case description. The features used (top-up behavior, days since data usage, complaint history) are directly relevant churn predictors. There is no reason to expect the top-2% performance to diverge from global AUC improvement in this feature space.

Point 1: Remains empirically contested. The defender's rebuttal is reasonable but the critic's concern is structurally valid. Force-resolved as empirical_test_required — compute top-2% metrics.

Point 3 remains open if no holdout was used. Force-resolved as empirical_test_required — if no experimental holdout exists, note it as a limitation and recommend A/B test at deployment.

Final verdict: empirical_test_agreed

empirical_test.measure: precision and lift at top-2% threshold on held-out test month, plus incremental retention if A/B holdout data exists
empirical_test.success_criterion: new model achieves materially higher precision and lift at the 2% cutoff than the incumbent; retention lift is positive among called users
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top-2% precision and lift are not improved relative to incumbent despite AUC gain, or retention lift is not distinguishable from zero

points_resolved: no_capacity_constrained_evaluation (acknowledged as a gap, easy to fill — computable from test data)
